notes.note: Gaius Vestorius, a banker and businessman of Puteoli who handled Cicero's local financial and building affairs; his freedman here brings a bundle of Atticus's letters.
Income-bearing property Cicero had inherited under the will of M. Cluvius of Puteoli; he tracks its yield closely (cf. 14.10, where it nears a hundred thousand sesterces).

A builder or architect named Chrysippus (not the Stoic philosopher of the same name punned on just below) summoned to deal with Cicero's collapsing rental shops; no registry entity could be bound with confidence.

notes.anchor: O Socrates, and ye Socratic men
notes.note: A mock-philosophical apostrophe: Cicero affects Socratic indifference to the loss of his shops, thanking the philosophers for teaching him to despise such material misfortune.
M. Curtius, who reproaches Cicero and his circle for tamely going on living under the surviving Caesarian order; otherwise little known.
Q. Caecilius Bassus, the Pompeian who had seized Syria and held out at Apamea; the besieger ("by him") is the Caesarian commander, and the Parthian relief under Pacorus is the news Vetus reports.
Pacorus, son of the Parthian king Orodes II; his forces relieved the besieged Bassus, fuelling Cicero's fear that a Parthian war was imminent.

The Volcatius blamed for the failure before Apamea is most likely L. Volcacius Tullus, a commander in the Syrian theatre, but none of the registry's Volcatii can be matched to him with confidence, so the surface is left unbound.

notes.anchor: sent envoys to Aurelius, who has been put in charge by Hirtius
notes.note: An officer named Aurelius placed in command in Gaul under the consul-designate A. Hirtius; he cannot be identified with any registered Aurelius, so the surface is left unbound.
"Calvena" ("Baldy") is Cicero's mocking nickname for the Caesarian C. Matius; his gloomy forecasts are here contradicted by Balbus's peaceful news from Gaul.
